data.title: NewLake Capital Partners: An Undervalued REIT With Double-Digit Yield
data.description:
Investment Thesis

NewLake Capital Partners (NLCP) is one of my favorite REIT stocks. NLCP provides real estate for cannabis companies for cultivation through long-term triple leases (10-20 years). NewLake Capital Partners is ranked number 1 among 13 REIT companies by Seeking Alpha Quant Ranking. It is a high-yield REIT stock with an approximate 11% yield. President Donald Trump has recently signed an executive order that will help to increase the research on cannabis and cannabidiol (CBD). This will reclassify marijuana to a Schedule-3 drug from a Schedule-1 drug. This will help in the research on the effect of cannabis as a painkiller and antidepressant on the human body. Though recreational use is still prohibited and comes under the same category as other illegal drugs. ​In my previous article I was bullish on NLCP and I still keep my “Strong Buy” rating.

Third Quarter Performance

Newlake’s revenue remained flat during the third quarter of FY25. The company announced a revenue of $12.6 million, reflecting no change on a YoY basis. Net income of the company slightly improved to $6.7 million in 3Q25 as compared to $6.3 million in 3Q24. NLCP’s surge in revenue is based on the new property acquisitions. On the contrary, a decline in the revenue reflects that a tenant defaulted. Since the revenue remained flat, which shows no acquisitions done by the company, but also no existing tenants defaulted either, which is a good sign.

Funds From Operations (FFO) were $10.7 million in the third quarter of FY25, while Adjusted Funds From Operations (AFFO) were $11.0 million in 3Q25.

Stable AFFO means that the rents were collected on time, no tenant defaulted, no tenant had material delinquencies, and dividends were paid on time by the company to its shareholders. Stability in cannabis REITs is rare. Stability in cannabis REITs is itself a positive sign, and that's why I am bullish on NLCP stock.

The demand for cannabis in the US market peaked in the past 30-35 years, especially since 2015. A study by Wiley Library – Society for the Study of Addiction, shows that from 1992 to 2022, the number of people consuming marijuana on a daily or near daily (DND) basis increased by 20x from 0.9 million to 17.17 million users. I believe this will play a key role in the flourishing of companies like NLCP, as the perception of US citizens has clearly shifted in the past 3 decades and especially the last decade. Data from 2023 by the U.S. Census Bureau shows that 88% American adults support the adult use of cannabis, while 54% American adults believe that alcohol is more harmful than cannabis. I think the statistical data shows a clear picture of the demand and once the legalities around cannabis are resolved, companies like NLCP will reap the benefit of it.

Another reason that makes me bullish on NLCP stock is that just 3% of its leases will expire in the next 6 years. 34% of its leases will expire in 2034 and 58% will expire thereafter. This means that more than half of its current leases will still be there even after 10 years and I am expecting that with current demand and perception of the general populace, I think the company will expand its cultivation assets and dispensaries and issue more leases.

Attractive Double-Digit Yield

​Another reason that makes me bullish on NLCP stock is that it has been consistently delivering value to shareholders with high yields. To me, this makes it an attractive choice for income-seeking investors looking for a high-quality dividend stock with a robust balance sheet. In 4Q21, the company was paying a dividend of $0.31, and in the last quarter, the company paid a dividend of $0.43. The company on 15 Dec, 2025 announced a dividend of $0.43 for the fourth quarter of FY25. The following data shows NLCP’s commitment to deliver value to its investors by consistently increasing its dividends over the years.

By comparing its dividend yield with its competitors in the industry, I can conclude that its yield is better than most of its top competitors in the industry. Only one stock, Innovative Industrial Properties, Inc. (IIPR) is outperforming, but I would still rate NLCP a better stock than IIPR. The reason I have is that NLCP has a very low debt of $7.6 million, and IIPR’s debt is $340.9 million. Also, revenue growth estimates of NLCP are extremely better than IIPR. 5 year CAGR of NLCP is 112%, and IIPR is 23%, making NLCP my top REIT stock.

What to Expect in 4Q25?

According to Seeking Alpha’s estimates, NLCP’s revenue is expected to remain flat. No significant changes are expected. If it is not increasing, it's not declining either, as it means that none of its tenants defaulted. This is in itself a good sign. Its tenants are paying rents on time, which keeps the earnings consistent in the 2nd to 4th quarter. It increases rent on a yearly basis, so an increase in revenue is expected in the first quarter of 2026. Though estimates are low for 1Q26, if vacant properties are leased to tenants, revenue will increase.

Its FFO is expected to be $0.51. Since the last 3 quarters, NLCP has beaten the estimates. So it's expected to continue the same trend.

NLCP did not provide any outlook, so it's obvious that the revenue will not witness a significant increase. In the long-run I expect more leases to be issued, addition of new cultivation assets, and dispensaries. This will increase their revenue and market share and eventually increase profits.

Valuation

Valuation ratios of NLCP also show a solid picture of undervalued REIT stock. NLCP is trading at a trailing P/E non-GAAP 12.19x (sector median 30.71x) and forward P/E non-GAAP 12x (sector median 31.26x). This shows that the stock is trading at an almost 60% discount as compared to its sector. A gap like this in most of the cases means that a business has a structural weakness or earnings instability. But looking at the NLCP’s income statement, I can say that its rental-based revenue provides stability. I think the consistency between its trailing and forward multiples shows its earnings are not going to decline, and the NLCP stock is undervalued.

The most important multiples to know the health of any REIT are AFFO and FFO. NLCP trades at trailing and forward P/AFFO and P/FFO of around 7.5x. This represents a discount of approximately 50% compared to the sector median. For REIT investors, AFFO shows the actual measure of distributable income. An AFFO multiple this low means that the company has inconsistent cash flows or reflects an elevated risk. Yet, NLCP covers its dividend payouts by AFFO. In my opinion, the market is underestimating its cash flow quality and mispricing risk. In my opinion, at current valuations, investors will be acquiring stable cash flows at a discounted price.

The strength of the balance sheet further supports my bullish thesis. Having less than 2% debt/equity ratio compared to 94% sector median, NLCP operates at a significantly lower leverage than its peers in the industry. Most of the companies are burdened with heavy interest rates, but NLCP is operating as an almost debt-free company, significantly reducing refinancing risk and increasing long-term stability. If a company has such characteristics, in my opinion, it should trade at a premium rather than a discount. ​

Technical Analysis - Price Forecast

My technical analysis of the NLCP stock supports my bullish thesis. As I marked in the chart, at an earlier point, you can see its all-time low, which is also its support and buyer interest level. At this point, the market achieved its liquidity (at around $11.84), and after this point, the price sidewaved and started consolidating, and because the buyer's order was filled, it gave an impulsive move. This indicates it was the buyer's interest level. After this, it rose and took daily swing liquidity. When it hit daily swing liquidity, it was meant to hit its previous support or bottom. And then it created a double bottom, which in itself is a strong bullish pattern. At this point, it created an order block with a fair value gap (FVG) and volume imbalance due to high buyer pressure. There is a significant chance that it will rise again from volume imbalance or order block and achieve the target.

In layman's terms, I would say that you can buy this stock in two or three steps. You can buy a minimum number of stocks at this point i.e $16. After this, you can buy around FVG, which is around $13.01, and finally, when it hits the order block, you can buy a maximum number of shares at a price of around $12.13. After this, the NLCP stock is supposed to hit the target, which is around $21.

So my price target for NLCP stock is $21, and that's why I rate this one a “Strong Buy”.

Risk

There is no risk-free business in the world. Companies like NewLake Capital Partners are also prone to risks, although they are not directly involved in manufacturing/producing and operate on the leasing of assets. One of the main risks that I have noticed is the tenants' indebtedness. Most of its tenants have a significant amount of debt and have expensive financing. This could lead to rent delays and could potentially result in the default of the tenants. In such circumstances, NLCP will also face a decline in revenue as most of its revenue comes from rents. Also, another risk associated with this is that most of its rent comes from a few tenants. So basically, they are NLCP’s large accounts. Problems with one tenant could potentially severely hurt NLCP’s earnings.

Regulatory risk is also looming over the cannabis businesses. As cannabis is federally illegal in the U.S., delays in regulation or unfavorable policies could result in the decline of tenants’ profitability.

REIT compliance risk is also a threat that cannot be underestimated. If the company fails to meet tax requirements, this could result in the loss of REIT status.

Conclusion

In my opinion, NLCP stock fundamentals are strong, the balance sheet is strong, and if you’re looking to expand your portfolio into REITs, NLCP could be the right choice. If you are an income-seeking investor, NLCP stock has a dividend yield of 11%. Once the cannabis market reaches maturity and regulatory restrictions ease, NLCP will reap the early mover advantage. There are risks as the regulations related to cannabis still pose a threat to the cannabis industry, but I believe in the long run, NLCP will be a good investment.

I keep my rating on NLCP stock a “Strong Buy”.

data.title: PepsiCo: Solid Momentum Coming Into 2026, But I Still Rate It A Hold For Now
data.description:
Introduction

In 2025, I parted ways with my PepsiCo, Inc. (PEP) position. Since then, the stock has rallied due to a solid Q4 earnings report, giving the Dividend King momentum coming into the new year.

While I like their improvements, their dividend coverage is still something that concerns me. But management seems confident in the company's ability to sustain its dividend going forward. This is evidenced by the solid 4% dividend increase.

In this article, I discuss Pepsi's latest earnings, fundamentals, and why, despite their improvements, I continue to rate the stock a Hold for now.

Previous Hold Thesis

I last covered Pepsi back in October after their Q3 earnings. Despite the beat, declining operating and gross profit margins signaled headwinds still lingered.

Dividend safety was also a concern, as free cash flow failed to cover dividend payments during the quarter. However, strategic moves and cost-savings initiatives looked to position the company for future growth.

But I wanted to see sequential improvements before considering upgrading the stock. Since then, the share price has seen a strong rally, blowing past their price target of $153.38. PEP is up over 12% compared to the S&P (SP500), up a little over 5%.

Rally After Earnings

As mentioned earlier, Pepsi's share price rallied after earnings for a few reasons. But first, I want to touch on how the company did on earnings.

During Q4, the beverage giant posted a double beat. This was better than peer The Coca-Cola Company (KO), which missed revenue expectations by $250 million. This likely also contributed to the rally, as PEP has struggled in the past few quarters due to inflationary headwinds.

EPS amounted to $2.26, beating analysts' estimates by $0.02. Revenue of $29.43 billion beat by $370 million.

EPS climbed from $1.96 the previous year, while revenue grew a solid 5.6%. This was likely due to pricing power. But organic sales also contributed, up 2%. KO's organic revenue rose 5%.

EMEA saw growth of 5%. However, their North American segment offset this, down 1%. Food volumes also declined, down 2%. But beverage volumes managed to see a slight increase of 1%.

In my opinion, the decline in food volume likely has to do with inflationary impacts and the rapid adoption of GLP-1 drugs.

During the quarter, management touched on this, stating that they expect a broader adoption of GLP-1 drugs going forward. But acquisitions, product relaunches, and lower prices on products are expected to offset this somewhat.

Management stated that affordability is still an issue. But they have a strategy aimed at targeting lower- and middle-income consumers. This is the relaunch of products and lowering of prices. As a result, sales are expected to improve in the back half of the year.

2026 Outlook

PEP initiated their 2026 outlook, which also likely contributed to their price rally. The beverage giant expects 5% bottom line growth, which is solid for a company of their size. Organic revenue growth is expected to be slightly higher than 2025's 1.7%. For 2026, growth is projected between 2% and 4%.

Why I Reiterate My Hold Rating

Despite improvements, Pepsi still saw profits and margins decline. Operating profits fell 11% year-over-year from approximately $12.9 billion to $11.5 billion. As a result, margins declined from 14% to 12.2%.

On a positive note, the chart below shows margins are expected to increase in 2026 and beyond. However, this remains to be seen as macro environmental risks remain from inflationary impacts.

Earnings per share were also down slightly, $0.02 from $8.16. This was due to net income declining from $9.6 billion to $8.24 billion. As a Dividend King, seeing declining cash flows is troublesome.

On a positive note, management did raise the dividend 4%, from $5.69 to $5.92 on an annualized basis. While this does give me some confidence, this means the company needs to grow cash flows significantly to get the payout ratio to a comfortable level.

The $10 billion buyback program should increase dividend safety. This is expected to be completed by 2030. Buybacks will also likely lead to earnings growth.

And while buyback programs are great, this could also be an indicator of Pepsi's struggles to grow organically. Lower interest rates will likely provide tailwinds. But these will likely take another 6 to 12 months for consumers to feel the effects.

Meaning, there's a likelihood that PEP will continue to see headwinds, particularly in its North American segment. And as a result, margins will likely decline as well.

Dividend Safety

As mentioned previously, cash flows declined. Cash from operations declined year-over-year, as you can see below. But on a positive note, free cash flow increased due to lower capital expenditures.

PEP returned, as promised, $8.6 billion through buybacks and dividends. Shares outstanding were down from 1.378 billion to 1.373 billion. Using their 2025 annualized dividend of $5.69, PEP would need $7.81 billion in free cash flow to cover the dividend.

If you remove the share repurchases, PEP managed to cover the dividend. But this gives them little room for error. I prefer non-REIT companies to have a payout ratio at 60% or below.

This gives them a well-covered dividend. And it gives them room for error and acquisitions without having to increase leverage on the balance sheet too much. Speaking of balance sheets, Pepsi repaid $4.1 billion in debt.

Their balance sheet, consisting of an A credit rating, does give them flexibility. Long-term debt stood at $42.3 billion, with approximately $6.7 billion in short-term debt obligations.

Cash on hand was plentiful, with more than $9 billion in cash & cash equivalents. So, from a balance sheet perspective, PEP is in good shape. But an increase in leverage and tight dividend coverage are items to be cautious of going forward.

Growth Drivers

I touched on growth drivers for PEP going forward. In 2025, the company made strides with acquisitions like Siete & Poppi.

They also increased their stake in Celsius (CELH), and Elliott Management took a stake in PEP to make improvements. All positive signs, in my opinion.

Last month they also announced a key partnership with NVIDIA (NVDA) and Siemens to leverage AI to improve supply chain operations. So, PEP is doing all the right things to move the business forward.

Valuation

Because of their strong rally, I suspect any significant upside may be limited here. At the current price, Pepsi offers little upside to their price target. In the coming quarters, investors will likely be on edge to see if the beverage giant can continue to execute.

I agree with Wall Street, Quant, and other Seeking Alpha analysts that PEP is a hold. Improvements, dividend increase, and buyback announcement seem to be priced in here.

For investors who missed the rally when the stock was trading in the $130s and $140s, I would consider adding on a pullback in the $150s.

Using their expected EPS growth of 5%, this puts estimated earnings at $8.55 a share. This gives PEP a current forward P/E of 19.41x, below their 5-year average of 22.48x.

So, the stock appears slightly undervalued here. But as I mentioned previously, I don't see any significant upside until the company shows they can execute on expectations.

Risks & Takeaway

Despite improvements, inflation and tariff impacts will likely continue to weigh on profits and margins for the near term.

GLP-1 drugs also present risks, increasing the likelihood for food volumes to be suppressed.

At the current price, PEP's improvements are likely priced in, giving investors little upside in the near to medium term.

I would like to see how the company executes on buybacks as well as see improvements in their North American segment before considering upgrading them to a Buy.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: 50% In Just 5 Stocks: Why I'm Willing To Invest Big In High-Quality
data.description:
Introduction

I have to start this article with a confession that may trigger a few people and make sense to others. As I believe many of you know already, I manage a highly concentrated portfolio. Last week, my portfolio became even more concentrated when I sold Home Depot (HD), which had become my smallest investment, to buy more QXO Inc. (QXO).

Now, I’m back to 15 individual stocks that account for roughly 70% of my total net worth (the rest is my real estate investment in Albania). However, that’s not everything. My five biggest holdings account for more than 50% of my portfolio, which means they hold close to 40% of my entire wealth.

These holdings are (in order):

  • LandBridge (LB)
  • Texas Pacific Land Corp. (TPL)
  • QXO Inc.
  • Old Dominion Freight Line (ODFL)
  • Carlisle Companies (CSL)

If you think that’s nuts, I’m glad to have your attention, as I am not writing this article to push these investments to you, but to explain to readers why I am willing to run such a concentrated strategy. I will tell you how I think about wealth generation and, even more important, how I pick stocks.

After all, ODFL, for example, has been featured in many recent articles. That’s not because I need to “pump” my own holdings, but because it is such a great example for countless articles, regardless of whether it’s about my cyclical growth thesis, valuation assessments, and examples of why dividend growth sometimes trumps dividend income.

In this article, I’ll also be able to explain why these companies make for such great examples. So, hopefully, at the end of this article, you better understand my reasoning and hopefully have learned something that could help you in your own investment process.

And it won’t hurt if I explain why I like my top 5 holdings so much.

So, as we have a lot to discuss, let’s get right to it!

Why My Strategy Is So Unique

Let’s start this part by giving you some facts. The S&P 500 holds 500 unique companies. However, its ten biggest holdings account for roughly 40% of its weighting (see below), which is one of the highest numbers in its history, thanks to the Big Tech dominance.

Meanwhile, the German DAX holds 40 individual companies. Its biggest five stocks account for roughly 43%, as you can see below.

In other words, although I’m giving you just two examples here, concentration, in general, often occurs, especially in smaller countries than the U.S., where a few very successful companies often dominate the main index.

However, I am way more concentrated than this, especially if we consider that LandBridge and Texas Pacific are both landowners in the Permian Basin, which means I have roughly 25% of my portfolio in two very similar companies.

This also applies to the market. Because of its top-heavy nature, it has become almost like an AI-ETF, as I have joked in prior articles. The hyperscalers that dominate the index may all be unique, yet they are all trying to win big in what I expect to become a “winner takes most” AI industry.

That’s also one of my biggest criticisms and a reason why I prefer the equal-weight S&P 500 (RSP) over the market-weight S&P 500 (the “normal” S&P 500). So far, this year, that thesis is gaining momentum, as the RSP/SPY ratio below shows:

I would even make the case that this year, so far, has been a wake-up call for many younger investors who may have been massively overweight in software and related tech stocks. When these started to crack due to disruption fears, a lot of investors saw their portfolios crater.

Moreover, on top of my belief that I can reasonably diversify with just a few positions, I also like to go big into my favorite ideas. Although I could easily go to 40 or even 50 holdings based on my long watchlist, I decided against buying everything I like to put the most emphasis on my favorite ideas. I always base this on at least four questions.

  1. What is the main driver of returns?
  2. Why does it compound?
  3. What could break my thesis?
  4. Is this worth a top-5 weighting, or is it just a stock I like?

This may sound super obvious, but a high concentration needs to be earned. My top-five picks are all companies that I expect can help me reach my financial goals on their own.

In other words, if just one of these companies delivers on its long-term potential, it could meaningfully change my financial future.

That’s how much I trust them.

What I Am Looking For In A Stock

I already briefly mentioned four key questions I ask before I invest.

The answers to these questions are based on a scorecard that looks for a few key things, including business model durability. I do not want to own a company in my top holdings that I have owned for just a few quarters. For example, that’s why I do not invest in semiconductor stocks but in landowners like Texas Pacific Land, where AI data centers are a bonus, not a must-have for the thesis.

I also need pricing power. Inflation is one of my favorite topics for a reason, as I not only expect inflation to accelerate, but also because on a long-term basis, every pricing power benefit can yield big alpha.

This is one of the reasons why I do not own PepsiCo (PEP) anymore. As much as I like PEP for dividend investors, given my age (30), its growth profile was simply not strong enough. I also did not want any more exposure to the ongoing fight for shelf space at global supermarkets. It’s tough. PEP may be a great company (I’m bullish on it), but I want more pricing power.

That’s also why I focused on TransDigm (TDG), as this aerospace supplier has more pricing power than a defense contractor that deals directly with the Department of War.

Then there’s the reinvestment runway. I added this to my scorecard this year, as it’s a critical concept. For example, one of the indicators I care about is ROIC. That’s the return on invested capital. It measures how high the payoff is of company investments. However, a high ROIC isn’t enough. Altria (MO), for example, has one of the highest ROICs on the entire market (see below).

However, because smoking (it sells Marlboros among other products) is in a secular decline, there is not a lot of room to invest. That’s why it has such a high yield, as most of its earnings are returned to shareholders.

Old Dominion Freight Line has a yield of less than 1% and a low-double-digit payout ratio. That’s because most of its capital is invested in new trucks and valuable real estate (ODFL owns almost all of its distribution centers in very strategic locations).

Next up is balance sheet health. Although I have bought companies with credit ratings below BBB- (BBB- is the first investment-grade rating), I never buy companies that cannot withstand steep downturns. When I was younger, I invested in turnarounds, especially in oil and gas. Nowadays, I make no exceptions. Zero.

Then there’s another idea, which is optionality. That’s a fancy way of saying that I want companies that have more than one way to win. For example, Texas Pacific Land and LandBridge have tailwinds from oil and gas production royalties (both volumes and pricing), the booming water business, and potential data center deals on their surface land.

Last but not least, I want good management ( management I trust) and a valuation that offers attractive upside. As this may sound obvious as well, I have one thing to mention here, which is that valuations need to be taken with a grain of salt. Texas Pacific, for example, usually trades somewhere between 30 and 40x earnings, which is 50% to 100% above the current market valuation. However:

  • It has no debt.
  • It has a top-3 net margin profile in the S&P 500.
  • It has no major CapEx requirements.

In other words, no capital is wasted on interest payments, a big share of revenue is turned into earnings, and, because of no major CapEx, most of it ends up as free cash flow.

This brings me to the next part of this article.

Treat It As A Case-Study

Sometimes, people tell me they do like my stock ideas but avoid them because they simply don’t fit their strategy. These comments make me very happy, as it’s not my goal to push anyone into specific stocks. My goal is to provide food for thought.

That’s why I will now quickly walk you through my picks. However, while I will tell you why I like them, I will mainly tell you what the lesson here is for further research.

Texas Pacific Land & LandBridge

These two companies are very similar, as they both own land in the Permian Basin. That’s America’s biggest oil basin. They own mineral, water, and surface rights, which means they make money on everything that happens on their land and under the surface. There are no similar companies on the stock market while I am writing this.

As a result, they make money from oil and gas royalties without having to spend a dime on CapEx, as investments are made by actual oil companies like Chevron (CVX). Moreover, they have water rights, which means they can supply water for the drilling process. On top of that, they make money from produced water, which is an oil and gas by-product that needs to be either recycled or pumped back into the ground.

The cherry on top is that the surface business is increasingly booming, as data centers have started to figure out that West Texas (that’s where the Permian is located) comes with dirt-cheap power, plenty of undeveloped land, no stakeholders like heavily populated communities, and few regulations.

LandBridge has one data center company looking for a spot on its land. Texas Pacific has ownership in Bolt Data & Energy, a company founded by Eric Schmidt. Although I would still be bullish on TPL and LB if they were never to get a data center on their land, I am convinced that both will get data centers in the years ahead.

So, what’s the lesson?

  • I love the high-margin royalty business because high margins translate into plenty of opportunities to reward shareholders and grow wealth.
  • Companies with low CapEx needs tend to enjoy higher valuations.
  • Companies with a lot of optionality tend to thrive due to their ability to capture multiple tailwinds.

Old Dominion Freight Line

ODFL operates in the volatile less-than-truckload industry. That’s different from truckload, as trucks don’t drive for single customers from Point A to Point B. LTL is based on shorter distances. Trucks pick up goods from multiple customers, bring them to service centers where they are sorted and put on other trucks to be sent to their final destination.

Essentially, LTL is based on efficiently connecting a wide range of (mostly industrial) customers. While this is a very cyclical business, LTL companies can actually create competitive advantages. ODFL has done this thanks to the most efficient network in the entire LTL industry. ODFL has spent decades buying high-quality land in dense areas, which means it owns most of its real estate outright.

It also put a big emphasis on service quality. For more than a decade, ODFL has been the number one in service quality. This has provided it with pricing power.

Essentially, it built a network that paid off, allowing it to invest even more. That flywheel made it one of the most successful industrial stocks in history, as dramatic as that may sound.

ODFL is cyclical, but I like that fact. I buy it when manufacturing sentiment (i.e., the ISM Manufacturing Index) is weak. During a phase of a boom, I just hold it. Then, during weakness, I buy more.

The main lesson, to me, is:

  • Cyclical companies don’t make for bad investments as long as they are able to grow over time and generate shareholder value. If they fail to do that, they are nothing more than a cyclical trading vehicle. I don’t care for those stocks.
  • Companies with discipline can even grow when demand is weak. ODFL has used its sales team aggressively to win new deals in recent years. That’s why I expect the next upswing to be violent (in a positive sense).
  • Real estate is often a strong advantage if used properly.

Carlisle Companies

Carlisle is a construction supplier. It mainly produces supplies for the remodeling and maintenance of commercial buildings. It is an industry leader with an ROIC of nearly 30.0% and an adjusted margin of slightly more than 26.0%. Even better, it has a lot of options for future investments, as it uses M&A to add new products in growth markets like insulation.

This is a terrific business to be in, as more than 55% of commercial buildings are older than 35 years. This is an age where aggressive spending is needed to prolong the lifespan of a building. CSL services that market. In recent years, it saw headwinds from elevated rates and a housing/commercial market that was stuck.

Now, as it gets unstuck, massive secular pent-up demand is likely to hit its order books.

Moreover, in its 4Q25, it stuck to its Vision 2030 goals, which include at least $40 in EPS. At 20x earnings, this implies an $800 stock price, nearly twice its current price. While I’m not guaranteeing that, I believe CSL equity is way too cheap.

The biggest takeaways here are:

  • ROICs often beat “cheap” P/E ratios, as CSL shines when it comes to putting capital to work, and it has plenty of room to spend capital.
  • CSL is the result of massive divestitures, resulting in a full focus on its core business. It’s one of the reasons why I avoid conglomerates.
  • Reinvesting capital is often a better use of capital than high dividend payments.

QXO Inc.

QXO is one of my latest additions. It’s a new company founded by Brad Jacobs, the same billionaire who also founded XPO (XPO), United Rentals (URI), and other businesses that ended up changing the industries they operate in.

Essentially, his gameplan is straightforward, as he buys companies in fragmented industries. He then uses technology to improve them, boost cash flows, and buy more peers. QXO is his latest endeavour, this time in the building distribution industry. It’s a major customer of Carlisle Companies after it bought Beacon Roofing. That was its first big deal.

Now, it aims for $50 billion in annual revenues over the next decade, a goal that could be supported by its latest move, which is the announcement to buy Kodiak Building Partners, a company that I consider a perfect fit due to its footprint in roofing, waterproofing, and other areas that QXO already operates in.

Although QXO is super volatile and an experiment, I feel so good about it, as I have spent almost a decade learning about Brad Jacobs. I also know that this is one of the best spaces to use AI as a disruptor. QXO sees low-hanging fruit in a massively fragmented industry that mostly consists of older, private companies.

While QXO is riskier than my other picks due to execution risks, I feel really good holding this company. It also shows just how effective a strong M&A strategy can be, which Brad Jacobs has proven for decades.

Other M&A giants I like are Danaher (DHR) in healthcare, TransDigm (TDG) in aerospace (I own it), Amphenol (APH) in the industrial space, and Intercontinental Exchange (ICE) in the stock exchanges industry.

Now, before this article gets way too long, here’s my takeaway:

Takeaway

I have a highly concentrated portfolio because I believe in putting a lot of emphasis on high-conviction ideas. My top-five holdings represent my best ideas and my biggest theses, including energy, housing, secular growth in data centers, produced water, commercial remodeling needs, and AI disruption in “unexpected” places like construction distribution.

In my case, it’s not about being too risky, but about allocating capital to the companies that I believe have the best risk/reward to allow me to achieve my financial goals as fast as possible.

In fact, I am so bullish that I believe even one or two of these companies can allow me to achieve my targets over the next few decades.

However, for now, the biggest takeaway is the uniqueness that these companies bring to the table. We can use this in future stock research, as they all bring something to the table that we should look for in other investments, including high ROICs, competent management, a lot of room for aggressive investments (tied to ROIC), secular growth, and even optionality.

Needless to say, the goal wasn’t to get anyone to copy me. This is food for thought. In general, I have to say that my way of running a portfolio is unorthodox and should not be copied by most people, especially not risk-averse people who like to sleep well at night. Please keep that in mind.

data.title: Chart Of The Day 2/13/26: 'Flash Crashes' Still Plaguing This Market
data.description:
By Mike Larson

It happened again yesterday. A silver “Flash Crash” that sent the metal down 11% in minutes. Gold suffered a similar (though smaller) slump, while equities sold off all day long. That volatility is keeping traders on edge... and rightfully so.

Take a look at the MoneyShow Chart of the Day. It shows the intraday trading action in spot Bitcoin and silver, gold, and S&P 500 E-mini futures yesterday morning and early afternoon. You can see that this was a cross-asset-class move, even as the percentage declines were smaller outside of silver.

Bitcoin, Silver, S&P 500 E-minis, Gold (Thursday Intraday Chart)

(Source: TradingView)

What happened? Was it a broader “risk-out” move? Part of a big rotation trade into bonds? A reaction to recent AI-driven washouts in SaaS stocks and financial services providers, plus yesterday's wipeout in trucking names?

I didn’t see any great explanations myself. But I do know one thing: seemingly unprovoked sudden, violent moves in any asset class can shake confidence. When you see them in multiple markets, that’s even more concerning.

I’ve been saying for a while that the first quarter of 2026 - and maybe the entire first half - will be choppier than we've gotten used to. So have MoneyShow experts like Jeff Hirsch. With market action now validating that forecast, be sure to account for it in your trading plan!

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Originally published on MoneyShow.com

Past performance does not guarantee future results. Invested capital is at risk of loss. Please read the above performance in conjunction with the disclosures on the last page of this report. All performance and data shown are in US dollar terms, unless otherwise noted.

What's on Our Minds

For most of the past 15 years, US equities didn’t just outperform, they made their outperformance feel inevitable. That’s what the idea of “US exceptionalism” became in practice: exceptional earnings growth, exceptional valuations (that didn’t matter), and eventually, exceptional concentration of market returns. Which has increasingly narrowed to a heavy bet on AI.

A whole generation of investors has been weaned on US stock market exceptionalism. On a rolling three-year basis, the most recent stretch of US outperformance has lasted approximately 193 months, or more than 15 years—more than two-and-a-half times longer than any prior episode. Such investors may assume that the underperformance of US equities in 2025 was an aberration soon to be reversed, but market structure has changed from 15 years ago. While valuations of US and international markets were once similar, the US now trades at nearly twice the multiples of foreign stocks. The valuation gap has widened, in part, due to the rising market values of the largest “AI plays” in the US that represent a larger proportion of the overall market. By year-end 2025, the top 10 US stocks by market capitalization represented roughly 40% of the MSCI USA Index, up from about 18% in 2009. Outside the US, however, concentration rose only modestly, with the top 10 members of the MSCI ACWI ex US moving from roughly 10% to 13% of the index’s value.

That widening gap in valuations and market diversity matters because it changes what US investors are currently pricing in and ultimately betting on. At today’s starting point, continued US market leadership increasingly depends on a single premise: that AI will produce an extended expansion of growth and profitability, most of it captured by a select few US companies.

But AI isn’t a monolithic trade; it’s a capital-expenditure regime with two distinct camps of participants, and two very different risk profiles. On the demand side are the AI capex investors: hyperscalers and platform companies committing large portions of cash flow to build compute capacity at scale. On the supply side are the physical enablers of AI: the global ecosystem that makes its buildout possible, spanning chip foundries, memory-chip makers, equipment manufacturers, as well as providers of power management, cooling, and the specialized industrial infrastructure required to deploy and operate data centers.

Despite the size of NVIDIA, the US equity market is disproportionately weighted toward the first group: the hyperscalers and the AI platform cohort. Alphabet, Amazon.com, Meta Platforms, and Microsoft are the largest buyers of AI computing power, committing an increasing share of their cash flow to data centers and infrastructure. Their cumulative spend is widely expected to reach hundreds of billions of dollars over the next several years. This is the heart of the US AI narrative: if you believe large capex inevitably translates into even larger monetization, the US currently dominates those profit pools.

International markets, by contrast, are more heavily tilted toward the second group: the ecosystem of manufacturers and infrastructure providers. While the US-listed NVIDIA may dominate accelerator economics, it’s a fabless chipmaker, meaning it can’t deliver a single graphics processing unit (GPU) unless the likes of Taiwan’s TSMC fabricates it. Amazon, Meta, or Microsoft may want the biggest data centers with unparalleled computing capabilities, but not one gets built without power-management systems or components from the likes of Delta Electronics or Schneider Electric. The US AI leaders’ products and services sit atop a deeply international supply chain—foundries, memory, semiconductor equipment, precision manufacturing, and the power and thermal systems required to run AI models at scale. In other words, non-US companies may include fewer of the headline AI platforms, but are key physical enablers of the buildout.

Market Snapshot

  • The MSCI ACWI ex US index outpaced the MSCI USA index in the quarter and for the full year. Cheaper starting valuations, tariff and policy uncertainty in the US, and a decline in the US dollar supported better returns for international equities.
  • Canada outperformed this quarter, as Financials there benefited from an improving yield curve, and gold and silver mining stocks continued to surge. Emerging Markets were bifurcated: China posted solid gains over the year but declined in the quarter amid concerns over slowing domestic demand, partially offsetting strong returns across much of the rest of EM, led by South Korea.
  • AI-related capital expenditures lifted Information Technology returns, with gains concentrated in semiconductor and semiconductor equipment stocks. Utilities also outperformed, particularly in Europe, supported by rising data-center power needs and increased grid investment.
  • Health Care rebounded on strong performance from pharmaceutical and biotechnology stocks. Sentiment improved following agreements between several companies and the Trump administration to provide discounted drugs to the Medicaid program, reducing policy uncertainty.
  • Communication Services declined, dragged down by media and entertainment and weakness in several large Chinese internet platforms. Index heavyweight Tencent fell sharply during the quarter, despite continued growth in online gaming and advertising revenues.
  • The cheapest quintile of stocks outperformed the most expensive quintile by nearly 12 percentage points. For the year, the performance gap between these cohorts was a staggering 46 percentage points.

Although the AI bubble question will continue to be asked in 2026, there isn’t a simple answer to the question. Businesses investing in AI infrastructure need to build customer bases and generate enough revenue to justify their investments. If that revenue falls short of expectations, that’s bad not just for those companies but also their suppliers, for while AI-related investment demand won’t disappear overnight, it could slow. If that were to happen, the pain will show up first in the most cyclical parts of the industry: semiconductor equipment and certain parts of the manufacturing chain, then foundries and component suppliers, and only later in the more insulated platform economics.

Both US and international investors have a stake in AI’s growth. But if the AI cycle slows, at the stock level the US market faces a double sensitivity: rich starting valuations and a large share of index returns that hinge on a narrow cohort. Therefore, the US market is more dependent on AI continuing to surprise to the upside to justify its premium. Non-US markets would not be immune, particularly the aforementioned group of more cyclical suppliers, but they start from cheaper valuations and possess a more diverse set of growth opportunities wholly unrelated to AI.

In several cases, share prices lagged even as underlying earnings continued to grow, an important reminder that the pace of AI adoption and the trajectory of AI equity returns aren’t necessarily synchronized in the short run.

This is also where policy becomes more than a footnote. When valuations are demanding and market leadership is narrow, policy volatility carries a higher price. Fifteen years ago, the US was more exceptional in terms of being a haven for global capital. Then, it enjoyed an AAA sovereign credit rating and ranked top ten globally in the US-based Heritage Foundation’s Index of Economic Freedom, reflecting a policy environment broadly supportive of capital formation and business activity. By 2025, the US fell to 26th on that list. Elevated uncertainty around tariffs, regulation, fiscal direction, and monetary policy doesn’t need to completely derail the AI story to matter; it simply needs to raise risk premiums and widen the range of outcomes for the most crowded part of the market.

So how do we respond as investors, especially when diversification has felt like a penalty at times?

In our portfolio, we own many companies that are direct beneficiaries of the AI buildout: semiconductor manufacturing leaders and equipment suppliers, and companies exposed to the power and thermal infrastructure AI requires. These include TSMC, the world’s largest semiconductor foundry; Samsung Electronics, the leading memory producer; semiconductor manufacturing equipment suppliers such as ASML, Disco Corp, Lasertec, and Daifuku; and companies addressing AI’s substantial power and thermal management needs, including Delta Electronics and Schneider Electric. And while the US has the hyperscalers making massive investments in data centers, we own Tencent in China and Naver in South Korea, both of which are investing in data center infrastructure to support AI computation serving regional customer bases, albeit with significantly lower levels of capital expenditure commitments. These holdings have participated meaningfully in the upside of AI-related investment, while giving us exposure to the critical layer outside US mega-caps. The above-mentioned companies, representing nearly a fifth of our portfolio would be considered “AI plays” by market pundits— roughly half what the US index bet is, but above what our international benchmark holds.

We’ve also sought more diverse ways to participate in AI and digitization beyond the most cyclical parts of the supply chain, with software and services businesses that are applying AI to improve their products, automate processes, and expand margins. Results here were mixed in 2025. In several cases, share prices lagged even as underlying earnings continued to grow, an important reminder that the pace of AI adoption and the trajectory of AI equity returns aren’t necessarily synchronized in the short run.

The more useful question isn’t whether an AI bubble exists but rather where expectations are most stretched—and which stocks bear the greatest downside if the capex cycle cools. And the more actionable question is, where is the market’s dependence concentrated, and what is already priced in? US exceptionalism has become tightly linked to an AI capex regime, and the US index is unusually exposed to concentrated bets facing a wide range of outcomes. International equities, meanwhile, otter participation in the same structural buildout through a greater variety of business models, often at more attractive valuations, with less single-factor concentration, and with a wider range of non-AI sources of growth.

If AI delivers on its promise, global winners will continue to extend well beyond US mega-caps. And if the capex cycle proves bumpier than the market expects, diversification across the chain and across regions may matter more than it has in years.

Portfolio in Focus

Turnover in the portfolio was higher in 2025, at 24% nearly double the average of the previous five years, as we sought to capitalize on equity-market dislocations caused by sharp swings in risk appetite. Activity in the fourth quarter was less than the prior two quarters, but continued in the same direction: we increased exposure to Emerging Markets, further lifted the portfolio’s growth exposure by reducing Materials and Consumer Staples in favor of Communication Services, and added selectively to European defense following a pullback.

Over the past decade we have maintained an underweight in Emerging Markets—averaging five percentage points less than the benchmark—not due to a top-down view on the region, but as an outcome of our bottom-up assessment of growth, quality, valuation, and risk across the international opportunity set. While many EM companies can offer faster topline growth and higher business quality metrics such as asset returns and low leverage, those attributes often comes with a trade-off in risk—including country risk, corporate governance, and management capital allocation. When those risks are reflected in higher discount rates, fewer EM companies have historically met our risk-reward analysis relative to developed-market peers. Over the past several years, underperformance and compressed valuations relative to developed markets has created opportunities in stocks of a number of businesses where fundamentals remain robust, including CATL, Delta Electronics, Naver, SF Holding, and NetEase, as well as DM-listed but EM-focused e-commerce operators Sea Limited and MercadoLibre. Good relative performance in EMs and our additions pushed our EM holdings to roughly 30%, their largest weight ever in the portfolio.

Naver (NHNCF), one of South Korea’s leading search platforms and the country’s second-largest e-commerce business, illustrates the trade-offs. Naver is investing in AI capabilities that can expand its revenue opportunities across both advertising and e-commerce by following the same playbook that Alphabet and Meta used in Western markets—making digital advertising both easier and cheaper. AI-based targeting, measurement, and automated ad-generation tools will allow Naver to help small and medium businesses which historically have spent less on Naver advertising and e-commerce because they lacked the know-how to use it effectively and found the cost of using digital advertising too high. While these investments will dilute profit margins in the near term, they should improve Naver’s long-term profitability and free-cash-flow generation as products scale up, capex plateaus, and monetization broadens. Against that backdrop, Naver shares trade at a meaningful discount to global internet platforms in our modeling of fair value as well as on traditional metrics such as earnings multiples, creating an attractive entry point for the investment we made this quarter.

Another new holding this quarter is BAE Systems (BAESY). BAE, formerly British Aerospace, is among the largest defense contractors outside the US or China, with capabilities across air, land, sea, space, and electronic systems, and the scale to serve multiple geographies. Its intellectual property, long-standing government relationships, and track record of program execution have supported resilient profitability even through long years of restrained European defense spending. With indications of sustained higher defense budgets in Europe, alongside a push to modernize in the US, BAE’s order book has strengthened while profitability has remained steady. We were able to buy shares amid a broader sell-off in European defense stocks, as investors wavered over a potential Ukraine–Russia settlement that could reduce near-term order momentum.

Good relative performance in EM and our additions pushed our EM holdings to roughly 30%, their largest weight ever in the portfolio.

To fund the investments in these higher-growth companies, we reduced exposure elsewhere. We sold long-standing holdings in Linde (LIN) and BHP (BHP), reducing our Materials weight to less than 5% of the portfolio. Linde’s valuation looked stretched against its high-single-digit long-term growth outlook. Meanwhile, BHP’s growth outlook appeared to deteriorate, with rising Australia–China frictions increasing risks around iron ore sales to Chinese steel manufacturers, who have built ample stockpiles of ore, yet face moribund demand growth. We also sold German enterprise resource planning software provider SAP, whose demanding valuation discounts a degree of growth acceleration unlikely to materialize.

Overall, these changes increased the portfolio’s growth profile without a meaningful sacrifice in quality, and at more reasonable valuations than a year ago. We end 2025 with a portfolio that betters the index in cash flow generation, a key driver of long-term outcomes, with a portfolio-weighted median five-year cash flow growth rate of 12.8% versus 9.5% for the index. The consensus 2026 earnings growth outlook for the portfolio of 17.6% is also materially higher than 13.0% for the index. As has long been the case, our portfolio of faster-growing and higher-quality companies comes at a premium valuation, but after the last four years of crushing style headwinds, that premium sits near multi-year lows, (as shown in the chart above), offering an attractive outlook for the portfolio.

Performance and Attribution

The International Equity composite returned 4.4% gross of fees in the fourth quarter, not far behind the 5.1% gain in the MSCI ACWI ex US Index. For the year, however, the composite rose 28.4% gross of fees, lagging the index return of 33.1%. The portfolio’s emphasis on higher-quality companies, and consequently its light holdings of the cheapest cohort of stocks, hurt performance across the board.

Portfolio Attribution by Sector

Sector allocation was modestly helpful in the fourth quarter, particularly in IT and Consumer Discretionary, but stock selection detracted across most sectors.

Top contributors to relative performance:

  • Our Financials stocks bettered the index in the fourth quarter, led by insurers Ping An Insurance and Manulife, both of which have seen a reacceleration of new business in China and Hong Kong, respectively.
  • For the full year, we had good stock selection and a modest overweight in the top-performing IT sector, led by technology hardware and equipment companies such as Delta Electronics and Samsung Electronics, as well as semiconductor maker TSMC.
  • Strong returns from Industrials stocks CATL, the Chinese battery specialist who reported battery shipments and earnings growth rates at close to 40%, and Ryanair, the Irish low-cost airline, which posted record-high quarterly profits in the third quarter, helped full-year performance.

Top detractors from relative performance:

  • In the fourth quarter, our IT stocks lagged, as our software holdings trailed the very strong returns of the sector, dominated by soaring shares of Korean memory producers and other semiconductor-related companies. Samsung Electronics, which managed to increase shipments of high-bandwidth memory to NVIDIA in the quarter, rose, partially offsetting our lagging stocks.
  • For the full year, our Health Care stocks were battered by a more hostile political and regulatory environment developing in the US, the biggest profit pool for the global industry, and by further valuation compression for many of the faster-growing equipment and services companies such as Sysmex. Our hefty allocation to the sector also hurt.
  • In Industrials, Swedish multinationals Atlas Copco and Alfa Laval, along with France’s Schneider Electric detracted from relative performance for the full year, with each still among the more richly priced capital goods stocks, and with growth that has wobbled amid uncertainty for capital investment outside of AI.
